case_name: Abhimeet Sinha & Ors. v. High Court of Judicature at Patna & Ors. (2024)
passage: The Supreme Court examined whether prescribing minimum qualifying marks in the viva voce for Bihar Judicial Service recruitment violated Articles 14 and 16. The petitioners contended that such prescription was arbitrary and contrary to All India Judges’ Association (1992) and the Shetty Commission Report. The Court rejected this argument, holding that the High Court, under Articles 233 to 235, enjoys constitutional autonomy in regulating recruitment to the subordinate judiciary. Fixing reasonable benchmarks in interviews, if applied transparently and uniformly, does not amount to arbitrariness or discrimination. The Court distinguished between eligibility and suitability, emphasizing that while the former depends on objective qualifications, the latter involves a holistic assessment of temperament and aptitude essential for judicial office. Hence, minimum viva-voce marks were upheld as a valid exercise of High Court power ensuring merit and institutional integrity.
question: In light of the passage, which of the following best represents the ratio of the Supreme Court in Abhimeet Sinha & Ors.?
option_a: Minimum qualifying marks in viva voce are unconstitutional as they create arbitrariness.
option_b: Minimum qualifying marks in viva voce are valid if the process ensures fairness and uniformity.
option_c: High Courts lack authority to prescribe interview marks.
option_d: The Shetty Commission report has binding force over High Court rules.
explanation: The Court clarified that prescribing minimum viva voce marks is not arbitrary if the process remains transparent and consistent. It recognized that interviews serve to assess communication, temperament, and suitability, vital for judicial office. Therefore, fairness and uniform benchmarks safeguard equality under Articles 14–16.

question: The Court in Abhimeet Sinha interpreted Articles 233–235 to mean that:
option_a: Judicial recruitment is a legislative function of the State Government.
option_b: The High Court has a supervisory and consultative role in judicial service appointments.
option_c: The Governor is solely responsible for recruitment.
option_d: Judicial Service Commissions must fix written-exam marks only.
explanation: Under Articles 233–235, the High Court exercises control and consultation powers over recruitment and disciplinary matters in the subordinate judiciary. The Governor formally appoints, but the High Court’s consultation is mandatory. Thus, the High Court’s rule-making power for interviews is constitutionally entrenched.

question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
 i. The Shetty Commission recommendations were held to be binding on all High Courts.
 ii. The prescription of minimum viva voce marks is not per se violative of Article 14.
 iii. Articles 233–235 confer autonomy on High Courts in judicial service administration.
option_a: i only
option_b: ii and iii only
option_c: i and ii only
explanation:
(i) ❌ Incorrect — The Shetty Commission’s recommendations are persuasive, not binding.
(ii) ✅ Correct — The Court ruled that minimum viva marks are not per se violative of Article 14.
(iii) ✅ Correct — Articles 233–235 ensure High Court autonomy in judicial administration.
Hence, only (ii) and (iii) are correct.

case_name: Abhishek Banerjee & Anr. v. Directorate of Enforcement (2024)
passage: The Supreme Court dealt with whether the Enforcement Directorate (ED) can summon individuals under Section 50 of the Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 (PMLA) beyond their territorial jurisdiction. The petitioners argued that such summons violated Articles 20(3) and 21, and must comply with CrPC procedures. The Court dismissed these arguments, holding that the PMLA is a self-contained, special statute that overrides CrPC wherever inconsistent (Sections 65 and 71). It reaffirmed the validity of ED’s powers and upheld that such inquiries are administrative, not criminal trials.
question: In light of the above passage, which of the following best captures the ratio of the decision?
option_a: ED cannot summon a person outside their state of residence.
option_b: The PMLA, being a special law, overrides the CrPC in case of conflict.
option_c: Section 50 of PMLA is unconstitutional for violating Article 20(3).
option_d: All proceedings under PMLA must follow CrPC procedures.
explanation: The Court reaffirmed that the PMLA is a special law with overriding effect (Sections 65 & 71) and hence prevails over CrPC provisions in case of inconsistency.

case_name: Achin Gupta v. State of Haryana & Ors. (2024)
passage:
The Supreme Court in Achin Gupta v. State of Haryana & Ors. (2024) examined misuse of criminal proceedings in matrimonial disputes. The appellant argued that the FIR under Sections 498A, 406, 323, and 506 IPC was filed as a counterblast to divorce proceedings, containing vague and mala fide allegations. Invoking its inherent powers under Section 482 CrPC, the Court quashed the proceedings, stressing that the criminal process should not be weaponized for harassment.
 The Court reiterated that fairness and reasonableness in prosecution are part of Article 14 (equality) and Article 21 (personal liberty) — principles central to the rule of law.
question: Which constitutional principle underlies the Supreme Court’s decision to quash the FIR in Achin Gupta?
option_a: Rule of law and due process under Articles 14 & 21
option_b: Judicial immunity and separation of powers
option_c: Parliamentary supremacy
explanation: The Court reaffirmed that arbitrary or mala fide prosecution violates Articles 14 and 21, which together ensure fair procedure and protection from abuse of legal power.

case_name: Achin Gupta v. State of Haryana & Ors. (2024)
passage:
The Supreme Court in Achin Gupta v. State of Haryana & Ors. (2024) examined misuse of criminal proceedings in matrimonial disputes. The appellant argued that the FIR under Sections 498A, 406, 323, and 506 IPC was filed as a counterblast to divorce proceedings, containing vague and mala fide allegations. Invoking its inherent powers under Section 482 CrPC, the Court quashed the proceedings, stressing that the criminal process should not be weaponized for harassment.
 The Court reiterated that fairness and reasonableness in prosecution are part of Article 14 (equality) and Article 21 (personal liberty) — principles central to the rule of law.
question: The Achin Gupta ruling emphasised proportionality between individual liberty and legitimate state action. Which of the following cases first constitutionalised the doctrine of proportionality in India?
option_a: Modern Dental College v. State of MP (2016)
option_b: K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)
option_c: Anuradha Bhasin v. Union of India (2020)
option_d: Om Kumar v. Union of India (2001)
explanation: Om Kumar introduced the doctrine of proportionality into Indian constitutional law, requiring that state actions be balanced and non-arbitrary — reflected in Achin Gupta.

case_name: Association for Democratic Reforms & Anr. v. Union of India & Ors. (2024)
passage: In Association for Democratic Reforms & Anr. v. Union of India & Ors. (2024), a five-judge Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court struck down the Electoral Bonds Scheme, 2018, holding it unconstitutional. The scheme allowed individuals and corporations to donate anonymously to political parties. The Court ruled that the scheme violated Article 19(1)(a) since the voter’s right to know is an integral part of freedom of expression. It found the state’s justification of curbing black money disproportionate to the restriction on transparency. The decision underscored that transparency, accountability, and electoral integrity are indispensable to a functioning democracy.
question: Which fundamental right was primarily invoked by the Court in striking down the Electoral Bonds Scheme?
option_a: Article 14 – Equality before law
option_b: Article 19(1)(a) – Freedom of speech and expression
option_c: Article 19(1)(c) – Freedom of association
option_d: Article 21 – Right to life and liberty
explanation: The Court held that voters’ right to information about political funding is a part of freedom of speech and expression under Article 19(1)(a).

case_name: Aligarh Muslim University v. Naresh Agarwal (2024)
passage: In Aligarh Muslim University through its Registrar (Faizan Mustafa) v. Naresh Agarwal & Ors. (2024), a Seven-Judge Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court (4:3 majority) overruled S. Azeez Basha v. Union of India (1968). The Court held that statutory incorporation does not erase a university’s minority character if it was originally established by a minority community. It laid down criteria to determine when an institution qualifies as a minority institution under Article 30(1) and clarified that minority rights extend to pre-Constitution institutions. The ruling redefined the constitutional scope of minority education rights and reaffirmed secular inclusivity.

explanation: The case centred on Article 30(1), which grants minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice, free from state interference.

case_name: Amutha v. A.R. Subramanian (2024)
passage: In Amutha v. A.R. Subramanian (2024), the Supreme Court dealt with a marriage that had irretrievably broken down after years of separation. The wife had filed a false criminal complaint later found baseless. The Court granted divorce under Sections 13(1)(ia) (cruelty) and 13(1)(ib) (desertion) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, observing that false allegations amount to mental cruelty, and prolonged separation amounts to desertion. Exercising its equitable powers under Article 142, the Court granted complete justice by dissolving the marriage and closing long-standing litigation.

option_c: Sections 9 and 24
explanation: Divorce was granted on the grounds of cruelty [13(1)(ia)] and desertion [13(1)(ib)], considering the false criminal complaint and prolonged separation as valid grounds.

The wife had filed a false criminal complaint later found baseless. The Court granted divorce under Sections 13(1)(ia) (cruelty) and 13(1)(ib) (desertion) of the Hindu Marriage Act, 1955, observing that false allegations amount to mental cruelty, and prolonged separation amounts to desertion. Exercising its equitable powers under Article 142, the Court granted complete justice by dissolving the marriage and closing long-standing litigation.
question: “Irretrievable breakdown of marriage” is—
option_a: A statutory ground under Section 13(1)(ix) of the Hindu Marriage Act
option_b: A constitutional right under Article 21
option_c: A judicially evolved ground applied under Article 142 for doing complete justice
option_d: A defence to a divorce petition
explanation: The doctrine of irretrievable breakdown is not statutory, but judicially evolved under Article 142, starting with Naveen Kohli (2006) and applied in Amutha (2024).

case_name: Anees v. The State Govt. of NCT (2024)
passage: In Anees v. The State Govt. of NCT (2024), the Supreme Court examined the scope of Section 106 of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872. The appellant was convicted of murdering his wife within their matrimonial home. The Court reaffirmed that the primary burden of proof always rests on the prosecution, but where the occurrence lies within the exclusive knowledge of the accused, an evidentiary burden may shift under Section 106. However, the Court cautioned that Section 106 cannot substitute a defective investigation — it merely serves as a logical complement when exclusive knowledge exists.
question: The doctrine underlying Section 106 of the Evidence Act operates on which of the following principles?
option_a: Reverse onus of proof is constitutionally permitted in all criminal cases.
option_b: The burden of proof lies on whoever asserts the affirmative, regardless of circumstance.
option_c: Exclusive knowledge creates an evidentiary duty to explain, once a prima facie case exists.
option_d: The accused must disprove guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
explanation: Section 106 creates a limited evidentiary burden where facts are within the exclusive knowledge of the accused, but only after the prosecution establishes a prima facie chain of guilt.

case_name: Anjum Kadari v. Union of India (2024)
passage: In Anjum Kadari v. Union of India (2024), the Supreme Court examined the constitutional validity of the Uttar Pradesh Board of Madarsa Education Act, 2004. The Allahabad High Court had struck down the entire Act for allegedly violating secularism and Articles 14, 21, and 21A, and for conflicting with the UGC Act. The Supreme Court reversed that decision — upholding most of the Act but invalidating provisions that allowed the Madarsa Board to regulate higher-education degrees (Fazil and Kamil). The Court clarified that secularism cannot independently invalidate a statute; a law can only be struck down for specific constitutional violations or lack of legislative competence. The ruling also reaffirmed that reasonable regulation of minority institutions promotes substantive equality, not constitutional infringement.
question: The primary question before the Supreme Court in Anjum Kadari (2024) concerned—

option_b: The constitutionality and legislative competence of the Madarsa Act, 2004
option_c: The minority status of Aligarh Muslim University
option_d: Whether secularism is part of the basic structure
explanation: The case focused on whether the Madarsa Education Act, 2004 was constitutionally valid and within the State’s legislative competence, rather than on secularism in the abstract.

case_name: Association for Democratic Reforms v. Election Commission of India (2024)
passage: In Association for Democratic Reforms v. Election Commission of India (2024), a Division Bench of the Supreme Court (Justices Sanjiv Khanna and Dipankar Datta) examined the constitutional validity of the EVM–VVPAT system. The petitioners sought either a complete return to paper ballots or 100% verification of all VVPAT slips. The Court upheld the EVM–VVPAT mechanism, holding that it meets the constitutional standard of free and fair elections under Articles 324 and 326. It ruled that technological transparency and procedural integrity, rather than absolute manual verification, ensure electoral credibility. The Court also issued new safeguards — sealing of symbol-loading units and allowing limited technical verification on request by candidates.

option_b: Whether the current EVM–VVPAT system satisfies the requirement of free and fair elections

option_d: Whether secrecy of voting violates freedom of speech
explanation: The Court examined whether the EVM–VVPAT process meets the constitutional standards of free and fair elections, interpreting Articles 324 and 326.

case_name: Babu Sahebagouda Rudragoudar & Ors. v. State of Karnataka (2024)
passage: In Babu Sahebagouda Rudragoudar v. State of Karnataka (2024), the Supreme Court reaffirmed the limited scope of appellate interference with acquittals under Section 378 CrPC. Referring to Chandrappa v. State of Karnataka (2007), Rajesh Prasad v. State of Bihar (2022), and H.D. Sundara v. State of Karnataka (2023), the Court held that an appellate court may reappreciate evidence but must exercise caution — interference is justified only when the trial court’s findings are perverse or wholly unsustainable. The Court emphasized the doctrine of double presumption of innocence — every accused is presumed innocent until proven guilty, and that presumption is reinforced upon acquittal. Where two reasonable views exist, the one favouring the accused must prevail, preserving fair-trial safeguards and restricting State appeals to cases of manifest miscarriage of justice.

explanation: The Court examined when an appellate court can reverse an acquittal — only when the trial court’s findings are perverse, unreasonable, or wholly unsustainable.
